statementLines: With reference to the Annual Financial Statement in India, consider the following statements:
I. It is prepared by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India.
II. As per the Constitution of India, the President shall, in respect of every financial year, cause to lay the Annual Financial Statement only before the House of People.
III.When the Annual financial statement is not passed by the House of People then the Prime Minister submits the resignation of the Council of Ministers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Annual Financial Statement in India, consider the following statements: I. It is prepared by the Ministry of Finance, Government of India. II. As per the Constitution of India, the President shall, in respect of every financial year, cause to lay the Annual Financial Statement only before the House of People. III.When the Annual financial statement is not passed by the House of People then the Prime Minister submits the resignation of the Council of Ministers. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**Annual Financial Statement (AFS)**, mandated by**Article 112 of the Constitution**, is the official statement of the**estimated receipts and expenditure**of the Government of India for a financial year. Commonly known as the**Union **Budget**, it distinguishes**charged**and**voted expenditure**, and is laid before both Houses of Parliament by the President through the Finance Minister. It forms the foundation of India's financial planning and legislative control over public spending. 

Statement I is correct: **Under**Article 112**, the Annual Financial Statement (AFS) is the constitutional term for the Union Budget and contains the**estimated receipts and expenditure**of the Government of India for the financial year. The Department of Economic Affairs, under** the Ministry of Finance, Government of India, prepares the Annual **Financial Statement,**which is later presented to Parliament.**

Statement II is incorrect: Article 112(1) provides that for every financial year, the President must cause a statement **of estimated receipts and expenditure to be laid before both Houses of Parliament (not only House of People), **known as the Annual Financial Statement. Constitutionally, therefore, the responsibility is on the President, but in the working of the parliamentary system, the Finance Minister presents this statement in Parliament on the President's behalf.**

Statement III is correct: **When**the annual Financial Statement is not passed by the House of People, the Prime **Minister submits the resignation of Council of Ministers**. The annual budget is a money bill and if the budget is not passed in parliament, then it can be understood that the ruling party has lost its majority in house. It means the government has lost the confidence vote in the Lok Sabha and it has to resign.

statementLines: Consider the following expenditures:
I. Administrative expenses of the office of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)
II. Such sums as Parliament may by law provide in the form of grants-in-aid to certain States.
III. Salaries and allowances of the judges of the High Courts
IV. Redemption charges related to the debt raised by the Government of India
How many of the above expenditures are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following expenditures: I. Administrative expenses of the office of the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) II. Such sums as Parliament may by law provide in the form of grants-in-aid to certain States. III. Salaries and allowances of the judges of the High Courts IV. Redemption charges related to the debt raised by the Government of India How many of the above expenditures are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **India's Constitution classifies Union expenditure into**voted**and**charged**categories. Voted expenditure (such as ministry allocations and schemes) requires approval of the Lok Sabha. Charged expenditure is**non-votable**, though it may be discussed but not put to vote.** Option I is correct: **Article**148(6)**states that the**administrative expenses of the office of the CAG**, including all salaries, allowances and pensions payable to persons serving in that office,**shall be charged upon the Consolidated **Fund of India**. This ensures the**financial independence**of the CAG.** Option II is correct: **Under**Article 275(1)**,**grants-in-aid of the revenues of States**provided by Parliament**are charged **on the Consolidated Fund of India**. It says** such sums as Parliament may by law provide shall be charged on the **Consolidated Fund of India in each year as grants-in-aid of the revenues of such States**as Parliament may determine to be in need of assistance, and different sums may be fixed for different States.** Option III is incorrect: **Article**221(2)**provides that**salaries and allowances of High Court judges**are paid from the**Consolidated Fund of the State**, not from the Consolidated Fund of India. Only their**pensions**fall under**Article **112(3)d)(iii)**.** Option IV is correct: **Article**112(3)c)**classifies**Union debt charges**, including interest, sinking fund charges and**redemption charges**, as**expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India**. This ensures that loan repayment obligations are met without yearly legislative approval.

statementLines: A Urea subsidy scheme was introduced in this year's budget with an appropriation amount of Rs.

x. The actual expenditure on the scheme was Rs. x + y. Which of the following types of grant can the Government of India seek from the legislature to cover the over expenditure of amount y on the scheme?
questions.questionText: A Urea subsidy scheme was introduced in this year's budget with an appropriation amount of Rs. x. The actual expenditure on the scheme was Rs. x + y. Which of the following types of grant can the Government of India seek from the legislature to cover the over expenditure of amount y on the scheme?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Option a is incorrect: **A Vote of Credit is meant for situations where an**unexpected demand arises**and the**magnitude or indefinite character of the service makes it impossible to present detailed estimates**, as provided under Article 116(1)b).**

Option b is correct: **An Excess Grant is required when**money has already been spent on a service in excess of the **amount originally granted for that year**, under Article 115(1)b). This relates to**overspending on an existing service**, not introducing a**new service not included in the original Budget**. The Urea subsidy scheme was allocated Rs. x in this year's budget, but the actual expenditure exceeded this amount (Rs. x + y). When spending goes beyond the amount sanctioned by Parliament, the government must obtain**Parliament's approval through an Excess Grant**, which authorizes additional funds for an already approved scheme.**

Option c is incorrect: **An Additional Grant applies when, during a financial year,**a need arises for additional **expenditure on a new service that was not contemplated in the Annual Financial Statement**, as stated in Article 115(1)a).**

Option d is incorrect: **An Exceptional Grant is authorised for a purpose that**forms no part of the current service of **any financial year**, as per Article 116(1)c). It is typically**non-recurring**and**special in nature**, not a continuing service.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Contingency Fund of India:
I. It was created through an Act of Parliament.
II. It shall be held on behalf of the President of India by a Secretary to the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance.

III. The President can make advances out of this Fund to meet unforeseen expenditure, pending authorisation of such expenditure by the Parliament by law. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Contingency Fund of India: I. It was created through an Act of Parliament. II. It shall be held on behalf of the President of India by a Secretary to the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance. III. The President can make advances out of this Fund to meet unforeseen expenditure, pending authorisation of such expenditure by the Parliament by law. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct: **Article**267(1)**authorises Parliament to establish a Contingency Fund for India. The Fund was**not created directly by the Constitution**, but**created through the Contingency Fund of India Act, 1950**, enacted by Parliament under the constitutional power given by Article 267(1).Its purpose is to provide the government with**immediate access to money for unforeseen and urgent expenditure**, without waiting for Parliamentary approval. This ensures quick financial response during emergencies. Statements II and III are correct: As per section 3 of the the Contingency Fund of India Act, 1950 **the**Contingency **Fund of India**shall be held on behalf of the President by a Secretary to the Government of India in the Ministry of Finance, and**no advances shall be made out of such fund except for the purposes of meeting unforeseen **expenditure**pending**authorisation of such expenditure by Parliament under appropriations made by law. **

statementLines: With reference to the abolition or creation of State Legislative Council in States in India, consider the following statements:

I. Parliament may by law provide for creation of Legislative Council in a state if the Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution by a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two- thirds of the members present and voting.
II. A law passed by the Parliament for the creation of a Legislative Council is not deemed to be a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368.
III. A bill for creation of legislative councils of state can be introduced in Parliament only with the prior recommendation of the President.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the abolition or creation of State Legislative Council in States in India, consider the following statements: I. Parliament may by law provide for creation of Legislative Council in a state if the Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution by a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two- thirds of the members present and voting. II. A law passed by the Parliament for the creation of a Legislative Council is not deemed to be a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368. III. A bill for creation of legislative councils of state can be introduced in Parliament only with the prior recommendation of the President. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **A State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad), provided under**Article 169**of the Constitution, is the**upper house**in a bicameral State Legislature. It is a**permanent revising chamber**, with one-third of its members retiring every two years. Its composition draws from local bodies, teachers, graduates, and nominated members, ensuring**expert review **and legislative scrutiny**.**

Statement I is correct: **Article 169(1) requires that before Parliament can act, the Legislative Assembly of a State first passes a resolution recommending the creation or abolition of a Legislative Council. For the creation of a Legislative Council in a State, the** Legislative Assembly of that State requires to pass a resolution by a majority of the total **membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting**.**

Statement II is correct:**Once the State Assembly passes the required resolution, Parliament may**by ordinary law**create or abolish the State Legislative Council. The Constitution explicitly states in Article 169 that such a law**shall **not be deemed an amendment of the Constitution for the purposes of Article 368**, meaning it does not require a constitutional amendment procedure.**

Statement III is incorrect: **Under**Article 169**, a Bill for the creation of a State Legislative Council does not require the prior recommendation of the President.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:

I. If any question arises as to whether a Member of the House of the People has become subject to disqualification under the 10th Schedule, the President's decision in accordance with the opinion of the Council of Union Ministers shall be final.
II. There is no mention of the word 'political party' in the Constitution of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: I. If any question arises as to whether a Member of the House of the People has become subject to disqualification under the 10th Schedule, the President's decision in accordance with the opinion of the Council of Union Ministers shall be final. II. There is no mention of the word 'political party' in the Constitution of India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The**Tenth Schedule **of the Constitution, also known as the**Anti-Defection Law**, was added by the 52nd Amendment in 1985. It lays down the grounds for disqualification of MPs and MLAs on the basis of defection.**

Statement I is incorrect. **The Tenth Schedule**clearly states **that in case of any question on disqualification under it, the decision is to be made by the**Speaker or the Chairman **of the House,**not the President**. This is clearly provided under the Tenth Schedule. The President has no role in disqualification under the anti-defection law, unlike disqualification under**Articles 102 and 191 which are decided by the President/Governor after consulting the **Election Commission. **

Statement II is incorrect. **The term**"political party" **is mentioned in the Constitution. It appears in the**Tenth **Schedule **which refers to the role of political parties in determining defection. For example, a member is disqualified if they**voluntarily give up membership of the political party **on whose ticket they were elected.

statementLines: With reference to a bicameral State Legislature, the position of the Legislative Council is equal with respect to the Legislative Assembly in which of the following cases?
I. Approval of an ordinance promulgated by the Governor of that State
II. Participation in election of representatives of the State to the Council of States
III. Consideration of the reports of Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)
IV. Ratification of a Constitutional Amendment Bill
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: With reference to a bicameral State Legislature, the position of the Legislative Council is equal with respect to the Legislative Assembly in which of the following cases? I. Approval of an ordinance promulgated by the Governor of that State II. Participation in election of representatives of the State to the Council of States III. Consideration of the reports of Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) IV. Ratification of a Constitutional Amendment Bill Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.** Option I is correct: **The position of the Legislative Council is**equal**with respect to the Legislative Assembly** in **matters of approval of ordinances promulgated by the Governor of that State.**Article 213(2)(a) states that, Ordinance promulgated shall be** laid before the Legislative Assembly of the State, or **where there is a Legislative Council in the State, before both the Houses.** Option II is incorrect: **The**Legislative Council of a State does not participate**in the election of the**representatives **of the state in the Council of States. **According to**Article 80(4)**, the representatives of each State in the**Council of **States shall be elected by the elected members of the Legislative Assembly of the State**in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote.** Option III is correct: **The Legislative Council of a State also**enjoys equal power**as the Legislative Assembly of a State in**consideration of reports of the Constitutional Bodies **e.g. - State Finance Commission (SFC), State Public Service Commission (SPSC),**Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG), etc. Article 151(2)**states that, the reports of the**Comptroller and Auditor-General of India**relating to the accounts of a State shall be submitted to the Governor of the State, who shall cause them to be** laid before the Legislature of the State**.** Option IV is incorrect: **Under**Article 368(2)**of the Constitution, the ratification of certain Constitutional Amendment Bills is carried out by the**State Legislatures**. In a bicameral State, this power is exercised**only by the Legislative **Assembly**; the Legislative Council has no role in the ratification process.

statementLines: Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to the Public Accounts Committee of the Parliament of India?
I. It was first set-up in India based on the provisions of the Government of India Act, 1935.
II. Initially, before independence, it consisted only the members of the Lok Sabha.
III. The senior-most member of the Parliament is appointed by the Speaker as the Chairman of the Committee.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to the Public Accounts Committee of the Parliament of India? I. It was first set-up in India based on the provisions of the Government of India Act, 1935. II. Initially, before independence, it consisted only the members of the Lok Sabha. III. The senior-most member of the Parliament is appointed by the Speaker as the Chairman of the Committee. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**Public Accounts Committee (PAC)**is a committee of selected members of Parliament, constituted by the Parliament of India,**for the purpose of auditing the revenue and the expenditure of the Government of India. **Its primary function is to examine the audit report of the Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) after it is laid in Parliament. 

Statement I is incorrect:**The Public Accounts Committees (PAC) was**first set up in 1921 on the basis of the **recommendations of the Montague-Chelmsford reforms of 1919 (and not on the provisions of the Government of **India Act, 1935). **Initially, the Finance Member of the Executive Council served as the Chairman and this arrangement lasted until 1949, when the Interim Government's Finance Minister took over as the Chairman.**

Statement II is correct: Since its constitution in 1921 till **1954-55**, the Public Accounts Committee consisted of 15 **members who were elected by Lok Sabha **from amongst its Members. But**with effect from the year **1954-55**, 7 **members from the Rajya Sabha are also being associated with the Committee. **The Public Accounts Committee is now constituted every year under Rule 308 of the Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha. The Committee consists of not more than 22 members comprising 15 members elected by Lok Sabha and not more than 7 members of Rajya Sabha elected by that House in like manner. 

Statement III is incorrect: **Till**1966-67**, a senior member of the ruling party used to be appointed by the Speaker as Chairman of the Committee. In 1967, however, for the first time,** a member from the Opposition in Lok Sabha, was **appointed as the Chairman of the Committee by the Speaker. **This practice continues till date. Knowledge Base:**Other Facts Related to the Public Accounts Committee: **The members are elected every year from amongst its members according to the principle of proportional representation by means of single transferable vote. The**term**of office of members of the Committee does**not exceed one year at a time. **A**Minister is not elected**a member of the Committee, and if a member, after his election to the Committee is appointed a Minister, ceases to be a member of the Committee from the date of such appointment. The**Chairman of the Committee is appointed by the Speaker from amongst the members **of the Committee from Lok Sabha.

statementLines: Consider the following Parliamentary Committees of the Lok Sabha in India, formed to facilitate legislative work:
I. Business Advisory Committee
II. Committee on Petitions
III. General Purposes Committee
IV. Committee on Private Members' Bills and Resolutions
How many of the above are headed by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: Consider the following Parliamentary Committees of the Lok Sabha in India, formed to facilitate legislative work: I. Business Advisory Committee II. Committee on Petitions III. General Purposes Committee IV. Committee on Private Members' Bills and Resolutions How many of the above are headed by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The Parliamentary Committees are smaller groups of Members of Parliament (MPs) who focus on specific areas, allowing for more detailed scrutiny and discussion of legislative measures and other matters. These committees can be broadly classified into two types: Ad Hoc Committees and Standing Committees.  Option I is correct: Business Advisory Committee is responsible for managing the legislative schedule**of the Parliament. It determines the time allocated for government bills, discussions, and other business within the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha. The**Lok Sabha committee consists of 15 members including the Speaker as its chairman. **In the Rajya Sabha it has 11 members including**the Chairman as its ex-officio chairman.** Option II is incorrect:**The**Committee on Petitions**is one of the oldest Committees of the House, which was first constituted in 1924 by the President of the Central Legislative Assembly. The Committee**examines petitions **submitted by individuals or groups concerning various matters**in the Union List, affecting them. Each House of Parliament has its own Committee on Petitions. The Lok Sabha committee consists of 15 members, while the Rajya Sabha committee consists of 10 members. The**Chairperson of the Committee on Petitions in each House is elected **by the members of the respective committee (not the Speaker/Chairman of the Lok Sabha/Rajya Sabha).** Option III is correct: **The**General Purposes Committee provides advice and consideration on matters concerning **the House's affairs,**ensuring that all relevant issues are discussed and managed effectively. In each House, this**committee consists of the presiding officer (Speaker/Chairman) as its ex-officio chairman, **Deputy Speaker (Deputy Chairman in the case of Rajya Sabha), members of the panel of chairpersons (panel of vice-chairpersons in the case of Rajya Sabha), chairpersons of all the departmental standing committees of the House, leaders of recognised parties and groups in the House and such other members as nominated by the presiding officer.** Option IV is incorrect:**The**Committee on Private Members' Bills and Resolutions**is a Lok Sabha committee**responsible for managing bills and resolutions introduced by individual MPs. **It prioritizes these proposals, allocates debate time, and ensures they receive adequate attention. The**committee is chaired by the Deputy Speaker of the **Lok Sabha (and not Speaker of Lok Sabha) **and comprises 15 members, who are nominated by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. Unlike the Lok Sabha, the** Rajya Sabha does not have a separate committee for private members' business.** Instead, it manages these matters through its Business Advisory Committee, which handles both government and private member business.

statementLines: The actual strength of a State Legislative Council is determined by:
questions.questionText: The actual strength of a State Legislative Council is determined by:

options.a: Legislative Assembly of the State concerned

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Option d is correct: **The**Constitution of India has fixed the strength of the Legislative Council**of the State. According to**Article 171 **of the Constitution, the total number of members in the Legislative Council of a State having such a Council**shall not exceed [one third] of the total number of members in the Legislative Assembly**of that State: Provided that the**total number of members in the Legislative Council of a State shall in no case be less than **forty. **While the Constitution specifies these limits, the**actual number of members in the Legislative Council is **determined by Parliament. **Knowledge Base:**Composition of Legislative Council: **Of the total number of members of a Legislative Council-1/3 Members are elected by the members of the legislative assembly of the state from amongst persons who are not members of the assembly. 1/3 Members are elected by the members of local bodies in the state like municipalities, district boards, and such other local authorities in the State as Parliament may by law specify.
1/12 Members are elected by persons residing in the State who have been for at least three years-graduates of any university in the territory of India, or in possession of qualifications prescribed by or under any law made by Parliament. 1/12 Members are elected by persons who have for at least three years engaged in teaching within the State, in institutions not lower in standard than secondary school.
1/6 Members are nominated by the Governor from amongst the persons who have a special knowledge or practical experience of the cooperative movement, literature, art, science and social service.

statementLines: With reference to Money Bill in the Indian Parliament, consider the following statements:
I. It shall not be introduced in the Council of States.
II. A private member of the House of people cannot introduce such types of bills in the House.
III. As per the Constitution of India, a Bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it provides for imposition of fines or other pecuniary penalties.
IV. The Council of States can amend such bills once transmitted to it by the House of People only at the first instance.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Money Bill in the Indian Parliament, consider the following statements: I. It shall not be introduced in the Council of States. II. A private member of the House of people cannot introduce such types of bills in the House. III. As per the Constitution of India, a Bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it provides for imposition of fines or other pecuniary penalties. IV. The Council of States can amend such bills once transmitted to it by the House of People only at the first instance. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Money Bills, defined under** Article 110**of the Constitution of India, constitute a special category of bills that deal exclusively with matters listed in**Article 110(1)**, such as taxation, borrowing and expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.**

Statement I is correct:**As per**Article 117(1) of the Constitution of India**, a Bill or an amendment dealing with any matter listed under** Article 110(1) **cannot be introduced or moved without the prior recommendation of the President. Therefore, a** Money Bill can be introduced in the House of people only with the prior recommendation of the **President**.**

Statement II is incorrect: **As per Rule 65 (2) of Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha, if the Bill is a Bill which under the Constitution cannot be introduced without the previous sanction or recommendation of the President, the member shall annex to the notice such sanction or recommendation conveyed through a Minister, and the notice shall not be valid until this requirement is complied with.** This rule reflects that a Private member can **introduce such types of bills which require previous sanction of the President. **There are no explicit provisions in the Constitution or the Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha which prohibits the Private member from introducing a Money Bill. Whether the President will give the recommendation or any recommendation will be conveyed by the Minister for a money bill which will be introduced by the Private member is a different thing. Thus,** a private member of the House of the People can introduce a **Money Bill in the House. **

Note: A majority of standard books on Indian polity provide that Money bills cannot be introduced by a private **member. But based on discussion with faculties and reading the rules of procedure of LokSabha, the conclusion **that we arrived upon nowhere it is mentioned that a Private member cannot introduce such a bill, though there is **no past precedent. **

Statement III is incorrect: Article 110(2), states that **a** Bill shall not be deemed to be a Money Bill**by reason only that**it provides for the **imposition of fines or other pecuniary penalties, or for the demand or** payment of fees for **licences**.**

Statement IV is incorrect: **According to** Article 109** of the Constitution of India, The Council of States cannot amend a Money Bill at any stage. It can only make recommendations within 14 days, which the House of People may accept or reject. Hence, the statement that it can amend the Bill "at the first instance" is not correct.

I. If s/he is declared of unsound mind by a competent court
II. If s/he is an undischarged insolvent.
III. If s/he is under any acknowledgment of allegiance or adherence to a foreign State.

questions.questionText: Which of the following are grounds for disqualification of a Member of Parliament under the Constitution of India? I. If s/he is declared of unsound mind by a competent court II. If s/he is an undischarged insolvent. III. If s/he is under any acknowledgment of allegiance or adherence to a foreign State. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Disqualification of Members of Parliament**in India is**governed primarily by Article 102 and the Tenth Schedule **of the Indian Constitution. The grounds of disqualification help maintain the integrity, independence, and ethical standards of the legislative body.** Option I is correct:**Article 102(1)b) provides that**a Member of Parliament shall be disqualified if he/she is declared **of unsound mind and stands so declared by a competent court. ** Option II is correct:**Article 102(1)c) provides that a member of Parliament shall be disqualified** if he/she is an **undischarged insolvent. ** Option III is correct: **Article 102(1)d) states that a member of Parliament shall be disqualified**if he/she voluntarily **acquires the citizenship of a foreign State or is under any form of acknowledgment of allegiance or adherence to **such a State. **

statementLines: With reference to the Indian Parliamentary system, consider the following statements about a Private Member's Bill:
I. These are a mechanism through which Members of Parliament (MP), who are not Ministers, can propose their own legislation.
II. Generally the period of notice of a motion for leave to introduce such a Bill is one month.
III. Only four Private Member Bills have been passed since independence.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian Parliamentary system, consider the following statements about a Private Member's Bill: I. These are a mechanism through which Members of Parliament (MP), who are not Ministers, can propose their own legislation. II. Generally the period of notice of a motion for leave to introduce such a Bill is one month. III. Only four Private Member Bills have been passed since independence. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The legislative process in India involves different types of bills, classified on the basis of their nature, purpose, and the authority introducing them. Among these, the Private Member's Bill is a significant category introduced by Members of Parliament or State Legislatures.**

Statement I is correct: **A Member of Parliament who is not a Minister is referred to as a Private Member.**Thus Private **Member's Bills (PMBs) are a mechanism through which Members of Parliament (MP), who are not Ministers, can **propose their own legislation. **

Statement II is correct: The period of notice of a motion for leave to introduce a Private Member's Bill is one **month, unless the Chairman/Speaker allows the motion to be made at a shorter notice **

Statement III is incorrect: Since Independence, 14 Private Members' Bills ( and not 4 Private Members' bills) have **been passed by Parliament and received Presidential assent.** Moreover, none of the Private Members' bills cleared both Houses of Parliament after 1970.

statementLines: Which of the following are the special powers enjoyed by the Council of States? Council of States alone can:
I. initiate the resolution for the removal of the Vice President of India.
II. pass a resolution under Article 249 of the Constitution of India empowering Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in national interest.
III. pass a resolution for discontinuance of the Emergency proclaimed under Article 352 of the Constitution of India.
IV. pass a resolution recommending the creation of one or more All-India Services under Article 312.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following are the special powers enjoyed by the Council of States? Council of States alone can: I. initiate the resolution for the removal of the Vice President of India. II. pass a resolution under Article 249 of the Constitution of India empowering Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in national interest. III. pass a resolution for discontinuance of the Emergency proclaimed under Article 352 of the Constitution of India. IV. pass a resolution recommending the creation of one or more All-India Services under Article 312. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**The Rajya Sabha, as the federal chamber of India's Parliament, is designed to represent the interests of the States. Although the Lok Sabha is the more powerful house in financial and executive matters, the Constitution grants certain exclusive powers to the Rajya Sabha to maintain the federal balance.** Option I is correct.**The Constitution of India under**Article 67**provides that a**resolution seeking the removal of the **Vice President can be initiated only in the Council of States. **Although the final removal must be approved by both Houses, the process can begin exclusively in the Upper House.** Option II is correct.**Under**Article 249**, the Council of States may pass a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting declaring that it is necessary in the national interest for**Parliament to **legislate on matters normally reserved for the States**. Once such a resolution is passed, Parliament gains temporary power to make laws on any State List subject.** Option III is incorrect.**The power to approve a**Proclamation of Emergency**under Article 352 lies with**both Houses **of Parliament**. However, a resolution for discontinuance of Emergency under**Article 352,**can be taken up by**the **House of People alone and not by the Council of States. ** Option IV is correct.**Under**Article 312, **the** Council of States**has the**exclusive authority **to pass a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting recommending the**creation of new All
- **India Services**(such as IAS, IPS, etc.). Only after this resolution can Parliament enact a law creating such a service.

statementLines: In the Council of States, suppose two opposition parties have the same numerical strength and are the greatest in number in the house. In this context,
which of the statements given below is correct? as the Leader of Opposition in the Council of States.
questions.questionText: In the Council of States, suppose two opposition parties have the same numerical strength and are the greatest in number in the house. In this context, which of the statements given below is correct? as the Leader of Opposition in the Council of States.

options.a: The leaders of both the parties will serve as Leader of Opposition to the House.
options.b: The House will decide through voting as the leader of which party will be declared as the Leader of the Opposition.
options.c: The Chairman of the House can recognize the leader of any one of the two parties as the Leader of the Opposition.
options.d: Amongst the two parties, the leader of one which has a higher numerical strength in the House of People will serve

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Salary and Allowances of Leaders of Opposition in Parliament Act of 1977**defines the Leader of the Opposition in relation to either House of Parliament, as a "Member of the Council of States or the House of the People, as the case may be, who is, for the time being, the Leader in that House of the Party in opposition to the Government having the**greatest numerical strength and recognized as such by the Chairman of the Council of States or the Speaker of the **House of the People**, as the case may be."**Where there are two or more parties in opposition to the Government, having the same numerical strength, the **Chairman, having regard to the status of the parties**, recognizes any of the Leaders of such parties as the Leader of the Opposition and such recognition is final and conclusive.
